macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishna on Piggul and Matirin (Permitted Items)

micro_segments.title: Overview of Daf 44 and the Mishna on parts of sacrifices and piggul applicability
Explanation of matir (permitting element): blood and meat/entrails relationship in sacrifices
Piggul does not apply to the matir itself, only to parts dependent on the matir

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Deriving Piggul's Scope from Pesukim

micro_segments.title: Initial question: does piggul apply only to shelamim or to other sacrifices
Linguistic expansions: basar (meat) and zevach to include all sacrifices and birds
Inclusion of menachot and log shemen of metzora through kedoshim language
Why does the Torah mention shelamim if piggul applies broadly to all sacrifices
The limiting principle: piggul applies only to things with matirin like shelamim
Exclusions: kometz, levonah, ketoret, menachat kohanim, blood—all are matirin themselves
Debate on menachot nesachim: are they matir themselves or dependent on korban blood

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Alternative Requirement: Blood on Outer Mizbe'ach

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon restricts piggul: requires matir on the outer mizbe'ach, not inner altar
Exclusion of parim nisrafim and se'irim nisrafim because blood goes on inner altar

macro_segments.title: Analysis of the Bechor Category and Klal U-Prat U-Klal

micro_segments.title: Question: only bechor is eaten two days and intervening night like shelamim; what about other offerings
Answer: using klal u-prat u-klal from 'im he-achol ye-achel' to expand beyond just bechor
Gemara validation of using two adjacent klalim to permit rearrangement for drasha

macro_segments.title: Dispute on Log Shemen of Metzora: Piggul or Not

micro_segments.title: Gemara identifies the baraita as Rabbi Meir's position: log shemen has piggul when brought with korban
Rabbis' counterargument: log shemen can be brought separately after korban, so it's matir itself
Rav Yosef's answer: Rabbi's position on log shemen—it has a matir even when separate (the sprinkling)
Rabbi Yirmiah's objection: log shemen brought alone still lacks piggul even with sprinkling
Abaye's reconciliation: Rabbi Meir's position splits by whether brought with or without korban

macro_segments.title: Deriving Priestly Entitlements from Parashat Korach

micro_segments.title: Chatas ha-of: deriving that kohanim eat bird sin-offering meat from 'kol korbanam'
Log shemen of metzora: included in 'kol korbanam' despite being a unique component
Lechol minchasam: includes omer mincha and sotah mincha beyond typical menachot
Lechol chatasam: extends to bird sin-offering despite Torah not explicitly stating priestly eating rights
Lechol ashamam: asham nazir and asham metzora—priestly entitlements by analogy
Gezel ger (theft from proselyte): money given to kohanim is fully owned, not merely guest-consumption

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer on Piggul by Parim Nisrafim—Acts and Thoughts on the Outside Only

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's position: piggul applies to parim nisrafim only when both act and thought concern outside work
Examples: sprinkling blood or burning emurim must occur outside with corresponding thought about outside acts
Source: makish (analogy) from pesach about 'like the flesh of a shelamim sacrifice'

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer on Piggul by Parim Nisrafim—Acts and Thoughts on the Outside Only (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara rules like Rabbi Eliezer: halacha applies to parim nisrafim under his conditions

macro_segments.title: Discussion of Pesak Halacha vs. Theoretical Study—Relevance of Temple-Era Laws

micro_segments.title: Rava's objection: ruling halacha on parim nisrafim is hilchesa lemashiacha (law for messianic era only)
Abaye's response: Torah study itself (derosh v'kabel sachar) needs no practical relevance
Rava's follow-up concern: paskening halacha implies practical relevance; what does it matter for future
Tosafot's analysis: other halacha rulings on Temple-only topics; some have practical applications today

topical_tags.term: Piggul (disqualifying thought about future consumption)
Matir (permitting element that enables consumption/burning)
Shelamim (peace offering—baseline for scope of piggul)
Kedoshim (sanctified items—broad inclusion via 'asher hem makdishim li')
Olah (burnt offering—wholly consumed, no human eating)
Chatat (sin offering)
Asham (guilt offering)
Mincha (meal offering—wholly burned by kohanim)
Nesachim (libations of wine/flour accompanying sacrifices)
Log shemen (oil portion for metzora purification)
Metzora (leper undergoing purification)
Parim nisrafim (burnt bulls—blood on inner altar)
Se'irim nisrafim (burnt goats—parallel to parim nisrafim)
Emurim (specific inner organs burned on outer altar)
Kometz (handful of mincha burned on altar)
Levonah (frankincense paired with kometz)
Ketoret (incense on inner altar)
Menachat kohanim (free-will meal offering wholly burned)
Menachat kohen mashiach (mandatory meal offering of high priest)
Bechor (firstborn animal—eaten two days and night)
Klal u-prat u-klal (general-particular-general exegetical rule)
Basar (meat—broadening language from specific to shelamim)
Zevach (sacrifice—broadening from shelamim to all sacrifices)
Asher hem makdishim li (anything sanctified—widest inclusionary language)
Meilah (misuse of consecrated property—status of log shemen)
Rabbi Meir's position on log shemen and nesachim
Rabbis' position: log and nesachim excluded when brought separately
Rabbi (Yehuda HaNasi) on log shemen: has two components like sacrifices
Rabbi Shimon's requirement: matir must be on outer mizbe'ach
Rav Yosef's explanation of Rabbi's position
Rabbi Yirmiah's objection to Rav Yosef
Abaye's reconciliation: with/without korban distinction
Chatas ha-of (bird sin offering—priestly eating rights from parashat korach)
Bechor (firstborn—only thing eaten two days like shelamim)
Parashat Korach—source for deriving priestly food entitlements
Kol korbanam (all sacrifices—includes log shemen despite uniqueness)
Lechol minchasam (all meal offerings—includes omer and sotah)
Lechol chatasam (all sin offerings—includes bird chatas)
Lechol ashamam (all guilt offerings—includes asham nazir and metzora)
Gezel ger (theft from proselyte—money, fully owned by kohanim)
Mishulchan gavoa (eating from God's table—kohanim eating korban meat)
Asham nazir (guilt offering when nazir becomes tamei)
Rabbi Eliezer's position: piggul by parim nisrafim only with outside acts and thoughts
Makish (analogy)—connecting parim nisrafim to shelamim via pesach
Chutz (outside/courtyard—where outer mizbe'ach work occurs)
Pnim (inside/heichal—where inner altar work occurs)
Shechitta (slaughter—one of four avodot done outside)
Shirayim (leftover blood poured out outside)
Rav Nachman ruling: halacha like Rabbi Eliezer
Rava's objection: hilchesa lemashiacha—law only for messianic era
Abaye's response: derosh v'kabel sachar—Torah study value independent of practice
Tosafot on paskening halacha for Temple-era-only topics
